data.title: Crocs Q4: Post-Earnings 20% Rally Is Just The Beginning
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

I last wrote about Crocs (NASDAQ:CROX) in November, where I reiterated my “buy” rating with a price target of $131.

While the stock has underperformed the S&P 500 during this period of time, it is up 24% on its Q4 FY24 earnings, where its total revenue grew 3.1% YoY to $990M, driven by outperformance in its Crocs brand, while the HEYDUDE brand posted flat year-over-year revenue after several quarters of contraction.

Meanwhile, its profitability picture was mixed with its gross margins improving 260 basis points to 57.9%, while its operating margin contracted by 160 basis points to 20.2% as a result of expanding SG&A expenses to drive long-term investments.

When it comes to its Crocs brand, its Classic Clog remains the hero, while the company continues to innovate across its franchises and product lines that also include Sandals and Jibbitz to expand its market share. What is also important to note is that the management is doubling down on its International markets, where China now ranks the largest after its North American markets, with Q4 growth accelerating from its Q3 levels.

Meanwhile, its HEYDUDE brand is also showing signs of optimism with its revenue bottoming as it is successful in driving full-price selling.

Looking forward, the management expects revenue growth to slow in its Crocs brand while expecting tariff-related profitability headwinds. However, I believe that the tariff-related headwind is priced into the guidance and the stock price. Assessing both the “good” and the “bad,” I believe there to be room for at least 20% upside from its current levels with a price target of $134, making it a “buy.”

Crocs Brand: Classic Clog remains the hero, while growth in International markets accelerates.

Starting with the Crocs brand, it saw its revenue grow 4.0% on a year-over-year basis in Q4 to $762M, contributing over 76% to Total revenue. For the full year FY24, the Crocs brand grew 8.8% YoY, with its DTC (Direct-to-Consumer) channel growing at a faster rate than Wholesale. In the meantime, total volume sold increased 6% for the whole year, while ASPs (Average Selling Price) grew 2% to $25.52 as a result of favorable pricing, channel, and product mix.

During the earnings call, the management outlined the strategic pillars of its Crocs brand, which I believe is worth discussing below.

The first pillar is centered on driving brand relevance through icon iterations, where clogs make up 75% of their sales mix, as they saw sales grow 10%, led by the Classic Clog and outperformance in its International markets, while simultaneously introducing new clog franchises and reiterating a strong pipeline for 2025.

Moving on to the second pillar, the management is focused on expanding the market share of its Crocs brand outside of clogs through new wearing occasions. This is how the management breaks down its TAM (total addressable market) below, which it estimates to be $160B in total, consisting of Casual Footwear, Sandals, and Clogs.

With Sandals and Jibbitz (in their Crocs product pillar) consisting of 13% and 8% of their total business, they grew at a slower rate than Clogs at 3% and 6%, respectively. Particularly, when it comes to Jibbitz, the management is focused on driving deeper penetration in digital and wholesale channels, accelerating speed to market, and introducing fresh products with agility.

Its final pillar is centered on gaining market share across the globe. This is how the management segments its geographies based on its existing market share to drive relevant go-to-market strategies. The geographic segments include 1) Growing, 2) Emerging, and 3) Established.

While North America is its largest market for its Crocs brand, contributing close to 62% of revenue in Q4, we can see that its International markets are growing at a faster pace, and we should expect it to contribute a higher portion to Total revenue in the coming years. Particularly, when it comes to its Chinese market, it saw a growth rate of 64% YoY for the whole year, with the fourth quarter revenue accelerating from the third quarter as a result of positive comparable store sales, digital, and new store growth. In 2025, the management is looking to expand its international footprint in China, India, Southeast Asia, and the Middle East and believes that a majority of their dollar growth will come from international markets in the coming years.

HEYDUDE shows further signs of revenue bottoming as ASPs continue to tick higher.

Moving on to its HEYDUDE, its revenues were flat YoY at $228M, driven by outperformance in its DTC channel. This is better than what the management had projected in its Q3 FY24 earnings, where it expected the segment’s revenue to decline 3-4% YoY.

In my previous post, I had discussed that despite investor pessimism for Crocs’s HEYDUDE brand, it was starting to see early signs of revenue bottoming, as it posted a sequential increase in revenue growth of 3%, despite a year-over-year decline of 17.4%. In Q4, HEYDUDE revenue grew over 11% on a sequential basis, which I believe demonstrates the progress they have made in prioritizing brand health through a combination of better segmentation, pricing strategies, and content strategies.

Meanwhile, the company is focusing its efforts on building a brand community, where they have expanded their following on social channels, while assembling high-profile influencers to drive relevant brand engagement, especially among the younger female consumer, where they saw a 160% growth in new customers in the female 18-24-year-old demographic.

At the same time, it is also driving product innovation in their core Wendy and Wally assortment that continue to see growing sales traction through full-price selling with ASPs growing 7%, marking the sixth consecutive quarter of ASP growth as they stabilize their North American market while laying the groundwork for future international growth.

Tariffs to impact FY25 profitability along with slower revenue growth

Looking forward to FY25, revenue growth is expected to slow down to 2-2.5% YoY, which includes a $60M FX headwind. Breaking it down by brands, its Crocs brand is expected to grow 4.5% led by its International markets, while revenue from HEYDUDE is expected to decline at a slower rate of 7-9%.

From a profitability standpoint, the management expects the gross margins for the full year to be down slightly from currency headwinds and tariffs, while its operating margin is also expected to decline to 24%, down from 24.9% in FY24.

On the topic of tariffs, I believe it is worth diving into it in a little more detail. During the earnings call, the management outlined that its forward guidance includes a 10% tariff on goods imported from China into the US, as well as the anticipated additional 25% tariff on goods imported from Mexico, beginning in March, with the assumption that the tariffs will be in place for the remainder of the fiscal year. As a result, the management has embedded an $11M tariff-related headwind to gross profit, which is equivalent to 25 basis points to their margin rate as well as a 60 basis point headwind to their operating margin.

However, these are estimates, and the impact on the overall demand landscape is unknown. What I am trying to say is that should demand for its Crocs and HEYDUDE brands get impacted worse than expected, we may see its profitability further worsen in FY25. Having said that, the management is committed to maintaining an operating margin at or above 24% over the longer term, assuming the headwinds from currency and tariffs normalize.

Revisiting my valuation: Room for 20% higher

Looking forward, I will take the management’s forward revenue guidance for FY25 while taking consensus estimates for FY26 and FY27, which point to a slight revenue acceleration in the 4% YoY range. I believe that Crocs should be able to achieve the consensus growth targets, especially as its Crocs brand sees its faster-growing International markets contributing a higher share of Total Revenue, while its HEYDUDE brand returns to growth. This should translate to a total revenue of $4.5B by FY27.

From a profitability standpoint, I will also take the management's FY25 guidance for Adjusted Operating Margin at 24% and assume it will remain at the level over the next two years as the management has discussed, which will translate to a total Adjusted Operating income of $1.08B by FY27, which will be equivalent to a present value of $932M when discounted at 8%.

Assuming a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 8.5, which is less than half of the average price-to-earnings multiple of the S&P 500 companies, given the growth rate of its earnings during this period of time, we arrive at a price target of $134, which is 22% higher than its current levels.

I would also like to point out that Crocs is trading at the lowest forward price-to-earnings multiple of 7x its FY25 earnings, compared to its peers, which include Skechers (NYSE:SKX) and Deckers Outdoor (DECK), which are trading at significantly higher multiples. While it is true that it is growing at a slower pace than its competitors, it, however, has the highest operating margin of them all, as can be seen below.

My final verdict and conclusions

Although revenue growth in the Crocs brand is expected to slow in FY25, along with margin contraction at a company level, I believe that it is already priced in. Unless we see a material deterioration in the overall demand landscape, I expect the company to meet or exceed its FY25 revenue guidance, which should position it for at least a 20% upside from its current levels.

As a result, I will reiterate my “buy” rating with a price target of $134.3.

data.title: Alphabet May Be A Strong Buy Again
data.description:
The last time we discussed Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL), aka Google, it was around $172 and the stock made a solid run of over 20%, before the recent correction. Google's stock cratered by about 12% in the days following its mixed earnings announcement.

This decline appears to have provided an excellent buying opportunity, as Google's earnings report did not illustrate any considerable issues that would likely impact its earnings in the intermediate and longer term.

Moreover, Google's growth and earnings potential remains strong due to its dominant market leading position, and the company's stock appears cheap relative to other mega cap tech stocks.

Technical Image - Buy The Dip

Alphabet had a substantial pullback of about 12% recently. This appears like a healthy correction, as Alphabet's stock became considerably overbought around the $210-200 resistance level. The technical pullback came close to closing the gap around $180, suggesting that $185-180 is a solid support level. The 200-day MA is slightly below $180, strengthening the support point and potentially limiting the downside risk.

Alphabet's Revenue Miss Is Minor

Alphabet reported GAAP EPS of $2.15, beating the consensus estimate by two cents. However, revenue came in at $96.5B, a minor miss of $170M. Despite this disappointment, Google's search business posted a record $54.034B in sales for the quarter.

YouTube ad revenue climbed by 14%, and Google's cloud business expanded at a very healthy 30%. Google's cloud has outperformed rivals recently, making it a bright spot in Alphabet's business arsenal. Therefore, despite the slight sales miss, Alphabet's core businesses remain in great shape.

Google Remains Cheap

Google is very consistent in beating consensus earnings estimates. Despite its minor revenue miss, Alphabet still managed to top its EPS estimates. While Alphabet could earn about $9 this year, in 2026 Google could earn $10-11 (in a base case scenario). With the stock around $180 and potential EPS of $11 next year, Google may trade at only about 16 times on a forward P/E basis. This P/E ratio is relatively cheap, and it's substantially cheaper than many other big tech companies.

For instance (forward P/E ratio):

  • Microsoft (MSFT): 25-27
  • Apple (AAPL): 27-29
  • Amazon (AMZN): 28-30
  • Tesla (TSLA): 50-90
  • Nvidia (NVDA): 25-33
  • Meta (META): 22-25
  • Broadcom (AVGO): 27-33
  • Alphabet: 16-18

PEG Ratio Perspective (forward):

  • Microsoft: 1.8
  • Apple: 2.4
  • Amazon: 1.5
  • Tesla: 3
  • Nvidia: 0.7
  • Meta: 1.7
  • Broadcom: 1.5
  • Alphabet: 1.1

The Takeaway

Excluding Nvidia, which has an abnormally low PEG ratio because of its rapid EPS expansion projections for next year, Alphabet has the cheapest big tech PEG ratio of about 1.1. This dynamic illustrates that Google's valuation is inexpensive relative to its earnings growth potential. Google also has the cheapest valuation on a forward P/E basis, considerably lower than other mega cap tech names. This dynamic illustrates that Alphabet's stock may have been pressed down too considerably, and it may be around an attractive long-term buy in level now.

Where Google stock could be in the future:

Year 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Revenue Bs $395 $440 $485 $533 $588 $630
Revenue growth 12% 12% 10% 9% 9% 8%
EPS $9.50 $10.70 $12.80 $15.50 $18.50 $22
EPS growth 19% 13% 19% 21% 19% 19%
Forward P/E 23 24 25 25 24 23
Stock price $245 $307 $387 $462 $528 $570

The Financial Prophet

Alphabet's sales could increase at a relatively modest but healthy pace of low double to high single digits in future years. This dynamic could enable its EPS growth to expand about 15-20% in future years. We may also see Alphabet's depressed multiple expand to 23-25 or higher in future years. This powerful combination of healthy EPS growth and multiple expansion could enable Alphabet's stock to rise considerably as we advance.

On Wall Street, the average price target for Alphabet's stock is about $220, illustrating a potential gain of about 20%. My one-year price target for Google's stock is slightly higher, $245, illustrating the potential for about a 30% gain in the next 12 months in a base-case scenario. This dynamic makes Alphabet's stock a solid intermediate and long-term buy, in my view.

Risks to Alphabet

Alphabet faces numerous risks, but one of the most significant risks may be the increased competition from Microsoft Bing, ChatGPT, and other competitors in AI, search, cloud, and other crucial segments. Alphabet must innovate continuously to remain highly relevant in AI and in other highly competitive spaces. Alphabet also faces regulatory risks and potential anti-monopoly issues. Investors should examine these and other risks carefully before investing in Google's stock.

data.title: Agnico Eagle: Another Blowout Quarter
data.description:
All figures are in US Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day.

Q4 and FY2024 Results

Agnico Eagle released its Q4-24 and FY2024 results this week, reporting quarterly production of ~847,400 ounces of payable gold production, a 6% decline from the year-ago period. Lower production in the period was related to lower grades at Fosterville (depletion of its highest grade Swan Zone material), lower grades at Detour Lake, lower grades at Canadian Malartic, and lower production at Kittila (lower grades and recoveries), as well as lower production at Pinos Altos. This was partially offset by record production at Macassa, higher production from its Nunavut operations, which combined for ~211,600 ounces, and increased production at LaRonde.

Notably, and one reason for Agnico Eagle's premium multiple vs. peers, more than 91% of Q4-24 production came from Canada and Australia, which are two of the world's top ranked mining jurisdictions, with more than 97% of production coming from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions when including Kittila in Finland. This compares quite favorably to other large-scale producers like Pan American Silver (PAAS) with less than 35% of production from Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions (Chile, Canada) and just ~10% from top ranked jurisdictions like Canada, Australia, and the United States.

Digging into Agnico Eagle's results a little closer, LaRonde contributed ~90,500 ounces in Q4-24, which was up year-over-year at lower cash costs on the back of higher grades and throughput (~802,000 tonnes at 3.78 G/T of gold). Meanwhile, full-year production also increased year-over-year to ~306,800 ounces, easily beating its guidance midpoint of 295,000 ounces on the back of higher throughput which offset lower grades (3.62 G/T of gold vs. 3.83 G/T of gold). Notably, the company called out record throughput and production at LZ5 with ~24,300 ounces produced, benefiting from stockpiles and continued automation initiatives.

At its Canadian Malartic Operations nearby, Agnico Eagle produced ~146,500 ounces, a decline from ~168,300 ounces in Q4-23 because of lower throughput and grades, with its new Odyssey Mine contributing ~21,500 ounces and beating targets on development and production rates in Q4-24. Full-year production was ~655,700 ounces vs. ~604,000 attributable ounces in 2023 (only three quarters with full ownership), with 2024 production beating its guidance midpoint of 630,000 ounces on the back of ~20.3 million tonnes processed at 1.09 G/T of gold vs. ~19.6 million tonnes at 1.17 G/T of gold.

Moving to its smaller Goldex Mine, production came in at ~32,300 ounces (Q4-23: ~33,400 ounces) at solid cash costs of $859/oz for an operation of its scale. Meanwhile, full-year production was down year-over-year at ~130,800 ounces, with the impact of lower grades because of a higher proportion of ore coming from the satellite Akasaba West deposit. However, the operation continues to perform extremely well with another quarterly mill throughput record set in Q4-24 of 8,820 TPD and its annual guidance midpoint achieved (130,000 ounces).

Looking at Agnico's largest asset, Detour Lake, Q4-24 production came in at ~179,100 ounces, a dip from ~193,500 ounces in the year-ago period. This was related to lapping higher grades, with the offset of higher throughput of ~7.09 million tonnes in the period of ~28.4 million tonnes annualized. Notably, this met the company's target of delivering on the 28MTPa rate in H2-24 and it's worth noting that this throughput target had already been pulled forward from 2025 previously.

As for full-year production, Detour Lake produced ~672,000 ounces, a 1% decline year-over-year that missed its guidance midpoint of 690,000 ounces. Lower production in 2024 was related to lower gold grades (0.85 G/T of gold vs. 0.91 G/T of gold), lower recoveries, but primarily because of abnormal chipping of grinding media, which affected grinding efficiency.

While the miss on annual production was a minor disappointment, Agnico Eagle reaffirmed its goal to increase throughput further to 29 million tonnes per annum by 2028 and the future of this asset is as bright as any, with a potential path to ~1.0 million ounces per annum next decade if its Underground Project is approved, with average annual production set to come in closer to ~1.1 million ounces in the 2030-2034 period, with this asset set to generate ~$1.5 billion in annual free cash flow at spot prices ($2,900/oz) post expansion.

Moving south to its Macassa Mine, the asset had a phenomenal Q4-24 performance, with record quarterly throughput and gold production - producing ~76,400 ounces in the period (+26% year-over-year) at industry-leading cash costs of $708/oz. This was driven by better productivity from a larger workforce, new ventilation infrastructure and better equipment availability, as well as contribution from the Macassa near surface deposit. The company noted that it's working on projects to further improve mill throughput, including improving ore grind classification and load in the grinding circuit.

As for full-year performance, Macassa produced ~279,400 ounces, a 22% increase year-over-year, and beat its guidance midpoint of 275,000 ounces. This was helped by higher throughput (~574,000 tonnes vs. ~442,000 tonnes) at slightly lower grades (15.55 G/T of gold vs. 16.47 G/T of gold), but still making this one of the highest-grade gold mines globally. It's also worth noting that Macassa is gearing up for another strong year and higher production at 310,000 ounces (midpoint) even with the impact of Amalgamated Kirkland [AK] ore deliveries to LZ5 pushed out to Q4-25 while it works on modifications at the LZ5 processing facility.

Looking at Agnico's Nunavut operations, Meliadine and Meadowbank combined for production of ~211,600 ounces, with output of ~94,600 ounces and ~117,000 ounces at Meliadine and Meadowbank, respectively. This compared favorably to combined production of ~205,500 ounces in Q4-23, benefiting from higher throughput at Meliadine (561,000 tonnes processed at 5.89 G/T of gold) and higher throughput and grades at Meadowbank (~999,000 tonnes processed at 4.07 G/T of gold).

As for full-year performance, the two assets combined for ~884,000 ounces in 2024, up from ~796,000 ounces in the year-ago period. In addition, both operations easily beat their annual guidance midpoints with annual production of ~378,900 ounces (370,000 midpoint) and ~504,700 ounces (~490,000 midpoint) at Meliadine and Meadowbank, respectively. At Meadowbank, this was related to higher throughput and grades (~4.14 million tonnes at 4.18 G/T of gold), with a continued benefit from positive reconciliation on tonnage. At Meliadine, we also saw higher throughput (~1.97 million tonnes) at higher grades (6.22 G/T of gold vs. 6.11 G/T of gold), benefiting from the completion of the Phase 2 Mill Expansion to 6,000 TPD.

As for other notable developments, Meliadine reported record quarterly haulage and development rates in Q4-24 on the back of optimization work, and its recently upgraded mill hit its target rate of 6,000 TPD in December. Just as importantly, Agnico Eagle received ministry approval of its previously submitted proposal to amend its Type A Water License at Meliadine to provide for inclusion of tailings, waste and waste management infrastructure at the Pump, F-Zone, Discovery and Wesmeg deposits.

Moving to Australia to its Fosterville Mine, production came in lighter in Q4 at ~37,100 ounces (Q4-23: ~49,500 ounces), impacted by lower throughput and grades of ~158,000 tonnes at 7.65 G/T of gold. Lower grades were related to the depleting higher grade Swan Zone, but also related to a 3.4 moment magnitude seismic event in November at Lower Phoenix. This seismic event caused damage to underground infrastructure, and Agnico Eagle noted that rehabilitation work is underway and it is adjusting mining methods, ground support, and its protocols to address seismic activity in deeper portions of the mine.

Despite the weaker Q4-24 performance, Fosterville still beat its guidance midpoint of 210,000 ounces, with ~225,200 ounces produced at $647/oz cash costs for the year. In addition, the company noted that its first stope was mined at Robbins Hill in November, providing greater operational flexibility for the asset. Finally, progress continues on the upgrade to its primary ventilation system, which is now at 92% completion. That being said, investors can expect a higher-cost year ahead at Fosterville based on its guidance midpoint of 150,000 ounces, impacted by lower grades (forecast: 5.83 G/T of gold) which is to be expected with the depletion of its highest grade Swan Zone.

On a positive note, Agnico remains optimistic it can get production back to ~175,000 ounces, with a potential improvement in production from the new 150,000 ounce base as early as 2027.

As for its Kittila Mine in Finland, production came in at ~51,900 ounces, lower year-over-year vs. ~61,200 ounces in Q4-23. Lower production was related to lower throughput and grades, with ~5.17 million tonnes at 4.15 G/T of gold processed in Q4-24, down from ~5.59 million tonnes at 4.55 G/T of gold in the year-ago period. The period was also impacted by softer recoveries, with higher carbon and sulphur content in the ore. Unfortunately, this weaker Q4-24 performance resulted in Kittila missing its guidance midpoint of 230,000 ounces, with ~218,900 ounces produced in 2024 at $1,031/oz cash costs.

Finally, Agnico Eagle's Pinos Altos Mine in Mexico had a softer quarter and year, producing ~18,600 ounces and ~88,400 ounces in Q4-24 and 2024, respectively. This came in below its guidance midpoint of 102,500 ounces for the year, and cash costs of $1,921/oz in Q4-24 weighed on Agnico's cost performance slightly in the quarter when combined with higher costs at Kittila. Still, with 7/10 of Agnico Eagle's operations beating their annual guidance midpoint and impressive cost control, it was a solid year overall for the company.

Track Record and Financial Results

As for its delivery against annual guidance, Agnico Eagle reported its second consecutive year of beating its annual guidance midpoint, producing ~3.49 million ounces of gold vs. a guidance midpoint of 3.45 million ounces for 2024. Importantly, this has given Agnico Eagle one of the best track records of delivery against its annual guidance midpoint vs. its peers, with an average beat of ~30,900 ounces in the 2021-2024 period or ~1.1% vs. its guidance midpoint, with 2020 excluded because of pandemic-related impacts.

When combined with Agnico Eagle's incredible per share growth metrics, Agnico Eagle stands head and shoulders above its peer group from an overall track record standpoint and delivering on its promises and creating lasting value for shareholders.

As for the company's financial results, Agnico Eagle reported revenue of ~$2.22 billion in Q4-24 (+27% year-over-year), operating cash flow of ~$1.13 billion (+55% year-over-year) and free cash flow of $570 million or $1.14 per share (+89% year-over-year). These were phenomenal results and were achieved with a realized gold price of $2,660/oz, ~10% below current spot prices suggesting a monster Q1-25 on deck with the gold price averaging nearly $2,800/oz quarter-to-date.

This strong financial performance allowed Agnico Eagle to reduce its net debt to just ~$220 million (repaid ~$700 million in debt), repurchase ~1.75 million shares last year at US$68.54 and pay ~$670 million in dividends.

As for Agnico Eagle's full-year financial performance, operating cash flow soared to ~$3.96 billion (+52% year-over-year), free cash flow increased to ~$2.14 billion (+126% year-over-year), and free cash flow per share, which is what should matter most to shareholders, jumped to $4.13 (+84% year-over-year). However, like its Q4-24 results, these results and ~26% free cash flow margins were achieved against an average realized gold price of $2,384/oz for the year, with the gold price currently hugging the $2,900/oz and setting up another record year for the company if gold prices remain at current levels.

It's also worth noting that this impressive free cash flow generation was despite higher capex in the period, with ~$1.82 billion in capital expenditures vs. ~$1.60 billion in the year-ago period, creating tough comps from a free cash flow standpoint.

Costs and Margins

Moving over to costs and margins, Agnico Eagle reported Q4-24 AISC of $1,316/oz, a 7% increase year-over-year but a much lower inflation rate than most of its peers. This allowed the company to report record AISC margins of $1,344/oz (50.5%), up 78% year-over-year or 1,240 basis points from $755/oz in the year-ago period.

On a full-year basis, Agnico Eagle's AISC margins soared to $1,145/oz (48.0%) and came in at industry-leading levels based on full-year AISC of $1,239/oz. This represented only a 5% increase year-over-year that easily outperformed its peer group and resulted in 860 basis points of AISC margin expansion vs. 2023 levels. However, even at a gold price assumption of $2,750/oz for 2025 and assuming $1,270/oz AISC, AISC margins should increase materially once again this year, improving to closer to ~54% or another near 600 basis point increase.

2025 Outlook

As for Agnico Eagle's 2025 outlook, it was a little softer than I expected, with 2025 guidance coming in at 3.4 million ounces at $1,275/oz AISC at the midpoint based on a 1.38 USD/CAD exchange rate where the company is 54% hedged currently. The expected slight dip in 2025 production is related to pushing ore from AK into late 2025 (now a 10,000 ounce contribution expected), and challenging ground conditions at Santo Nino at Pinos Altos that reduced its 2025 production outlook (80,000 ounces vs. 130,000 ounces previously). The other impact was deferring the restart of pre-crushing low-grade at Canadian Malartic to allow for a slower ramp-up of its in-pit tailings disposal.

The result was a ~3% impact on its prior 2025 guidance of 3.5 million ounces at the midpoint, or ~100,000 ounces.

However, it's worth noting that Agnico Eagle has typically guided conservatively, evidenced by a ~30,000 beat on average over the past four years vs. guidance. Hence, I don't think it would be surprising at all to see the company produce ~4.32 to ~4.34 million ounces this year, or an up to 1% beat vs. its guidance midpoint in line with its track record.

In addition, while it may look like production is stalling at current levels, this is hardly the case, with Agnico Eagle's incredible pipeline supporting growth toward 4.0 million ounces of gold with major opportunities like Canadian Malartic Fill-The-Mill & Second Shaft (1.0+ million ounce potential), Detour Lake (1.0+ million ounce per annum potential), San Nicolas (not yet approved), Upper Beaver, and Hope Bay which should offset most of the production losses from Meadowbank once it heads offline.

The other great news is that Agnico Eagle is in the enviable position of having extremely strong currency (share price), meaning it could look at external growth to improve its production profile even further at a relatively low cost (using well valued shares or a mix of its well valued shares + cash), supporting further growth in production and reserves per share if it pursues any additional M&A aside from the recent bolt-on deal for O3 Mining.

Recent Developments

As for recent developments, Agnico continues to discuss the potential for a second shaft at Canadian Malartic, given exploration success at Odyssey, suggesting the potential to increase long-term production to 1.0+ million ounces with the ability to leverage its ~40,000 TPD of excess mill capacity. Other opportunities to fill the mill aside from hoisting more tonnes from East Gouldie include Marban (O3 Mining acquisition) and Wasamac (Yamana Canada acquisition), with ore from Marban, Wasamac and a possible second shaft, if all approved, pushing production upward of 1.0 million ounces per annum.

Meanwhile, Agnico Eagle remains very optimistic about Hope Bay's long-term potential, with another busy year where it expects to spend just shy of $100 million to upgrade existing infrastructure and advance the site for redevelopment. This is expected to include dismantling the mill built by TMac Resources (prior to its acquisition), extending the airstrip and the completion of early earthworks. Agnico Eagle is also planning a 2.1 kilometer exploration ramp at Madrid (similar to its plans at Detour Lake Underground) to allow for infill/expansion drilling at Madrid and extended to Suluk/Patch 7 where it reported an indicated resource of ~900,000 ounces at 6.64 G/T of gold.

It's worth noting that Agnico Eagle acquired this asset (Hope Bay) with its 80-kilometer gold belt in Nunavut for a song in early 2021 for ~$50/oz with it now looking like this asset could support an up to 400,000 ounce per annum operation, above its initial expectations of 300,000 ounces/year. The same asset today in the roaring gold bull market we're in would likely transact at quadruple the price that Agnico Eagle paid or well over $1.0 billion, highlighting Agnico Eagle's ability to consistently steal value and its strong track record of opportunistically growing per share metrics while beefing up its pipeline.

Finally, Agnico Eagle shared that it's looking at the potential to extend Meadowbank's mine life based solely on underground production, with a study expected in H1-26 that will complement its planned study at Hope Bay also set to be released in H1-26. All that being said, and given the outlook for higher capex to support long-term production growth, free cash flow will be pressured a little next year given the increased spending across the portfolio. This is hardly a huge deal at all and these are extremely high IRR opportunities it's investing in, but Agnico Eagle will need a ~$2,700/oz gold price if it hopes to generate similar free cash flow to 2024 levels.

Summary

Agnico Eagle reported another year of near flawless operating results and, true to form, over-delivered on its promises. Importantly, it was another year of significant exploration success as well, setting this portfolio for growth to ~4.0 million ounces per annum longer-term. This combination of consistently delivering operational results better than its peers and delivering industry-leading margins with its ounces coming out of safer jurisdictions continues to make Agnico Eagle stand out relative to peers and makes it a premier name in the sector and the top-quality producer sector-wide.

Given this distinction, I continue to remain long the stock from ~US$46.00 and I would strongly consider adding to my position on any 20%+ pullbacks.

data.title: AMD: Stop Dreaming Of Big Returns
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) has essentially lost half its value over the past year, as the semiconductor giant failed to live up to the hype around its AI monetization possibilities.

Many analysts have recently doubled down on their bullishness following the rise of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK). The fact that the Chinese AI start-up trained their high-performance models on Nvidia’s (NVDA) export-compliant H800s has convinced investors that tech companies don’t necessarily need the most powerful GPUs available to train new Large Language Models (LLMs). This would supposedly enable AMD to capture more market share. Although the bulls may be getting ahead of themselves here.

In the previous article before its earnings report, the advice was not to buy AMD shares just because of its cheap valuation, given the rise of the custom silicon wave, with Broadcom (AVGO) emerging as the Number 2 AI winner after Nvidia (NVDA). In the preceding article, AMD was downgraded to a “hold.” We covered how the prevailing shift from the “training” to the “inferencing” phase was supposed to create an entry point for AMD, as less powerful chips are believed to be sufficient for “inferencing.” However, chief rival Nvidia has established an extensive installed base across data centers over the past few years, and has successfully induced dual usage of its GPUs for both training and inferencing, consequently narrowing the window for AMD.

Since then, more statistical evidence has come out that reveals increased utilization of Nvidia’s GPUs for inferencing, while the hyper-scalers are simultaneously prioritizing custom silicon deployments, aggravating AMD’s revenue growth potential, subsequently subduing its stock price performance going forward.

AMD stock maintains a “hold” rating.

AMD’s GPU Revenue Struggles To Gain Momentum

On AMD’s Q4 2024 earnings call, CEO Lisa Su shared that around half of the company’s annual revenue derived from the data center segment, which grew by 94% in 2024, making it an important determinant of stock price performance.

Strength in the data center unit was buoyed by impressive strength in its server CPU business. However, investors’ main focus remains on the future of computing hardware amid the ongoing AI revolution, which would be the GPU business line.

AMD had projected $5 billion in AI-related data center revenue for 2024, and investors assumed the executives were offering conservative estimates to be able to easily beat their guidance. But actual revenue seems to have come in line with these expectations, disappointing the bulls who were hoping for the company to beat the guided number by a wide margin. Moreover, executives also seem to have avoided offering a concrete AI revenue projection for 2025, and instead just offering a positive outlook for the long term.

We have made outstanding progress building the foundational product, technology and customer relationships needed to capture a meaningful portion of this market. And we believe this places AMD on a steep long-term growth trajectory, led by the rapid scaling of our data center AI franchise for more than $5 billion of revenue in 2024 to tens of billions of dollars of annual revenue over the coming years.

– CEO Lisa Su, AMD Q4 2024 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

Now that’s a very vague revenue projection, and does little to appease investors who are already concerned about AMD’s ability to capture more data center market share, which is the primary reason why the stock is down 47% from its 52-week-high.

Additionally, AMD’s executives shared on the call that they expect GPU revenue in the first half of 2025 to be flattish, and then pick up again in the second half following the launch of its new “Instinct MI350” around mid-year.

Contrarily, in the run-up to the launch of Nvidia’s new Blackwell chips, the AI leader continued to deliver attractive revenue growth rates as companies carried on purchasing H100s in large volumes. This was to equip their data centers with the most powerful chips available on the market.

Let’s consider this from the perspective of Cloud Service Providers (CSPs) as an example. CSPs want to build data centers with maximum compute capacity, but there are a limited number of rack server systems that they can fit into a single data center. So they are likely to prioritize Nvidia GPUs as they prepare for increasingly complex AI workloads (e.g., video-generating AI models and applications). The last thing these CSPs want is being in a situation where the technology has advanced tremendously, and new customers come to them with more complex workloads, but their servers are not powerful enough to handle these heavier workloads. This risks the loss of such clients to rivals. Therefore, while CSPs will allocate a slight portion of their data center space to AMD chips for diversification purposes, Nvidia’s GPUs will continue holding the lion’s share given its wide lead in computing power.

AMD Is Not Necessarily A Winner From DeepSeek’s Rise

Now the revelations of DeepSeek’s algorithmic innovations and model development techniques have sparked a debate over whether increasingly powerful GPUs are still needed, giving the bulls hope that AMD should witness growing demand for its chips going forward.

Tech experts have highlighted that DeepSeek heavily used a technique called “distillation” to develop its own models off existing LLMs. Now it is important to note that “distillation” is not some brand-new methodology, tech companies have already been using it for years. Moreover, one year ago on Microsoft’s Q2 FY2024 earnings call, CEO Satya Nadella pointed out that “distillation” is actually carried out on inferencing infrastructure:

you will also distil a large model to a small model and that would be a training perhaps. But that's a small batch training that uses essentially inference infrastructure

Now the bulls argue that DeepSeek’s success should push tech companies to re-evaluate their approaches to AI model development, and expect the hyper-scalers to increasingly prioritize techniques like “distillation” instead of building new models from the ground-up. This would accelerate the shift towards inferencing workloads, subduing the need for Nvidia’s powerful GPUs, and potentially supporting demand for AMD’s relatively less-powerful chips.

However, even if we see an uptick in “distillation” workloads going forward, it does not necessarily mean an immediate rise in demand for AMD’s GPUs, as discussed in a previous article:

Once customers are done with training their AI models using Nvidia’s mighty chips, these GPUs are then being deployed for inferencing workloads, subduing the need to purchase an entirely new batch of silicon from AMD.

Since then, more evidence has arisen that Nvidia’s hardware is being increasingly deployed for inferencing workloads.

UBS recently surveyed the executives of IT departments across 125 organizations, which revealed the great progress Nvidia has been making in inducing greater usage of its chips during the inferencing phase, while AMD lags.

Regarding AI hardware, Nvidia continued to dominate, with 66% of respondents using their GPUs for training and 63% for inference. Last May, only 50% of respondents preferred Nvidia for inference. Amazon Web Services' (NASDAQ:AMZN) Trainium chips ranked second at 17% and Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) were a distant third.

– Seeking Alpha News.

A key strategy Nvidia uses to augment the value proposition of its chips into the inferencing phase is continuously advancing the algorithmic software operations on its GPUs to improve performance beyond the capabilities enabled by the silicon designs. This extends the usability of the hardware over several years.

In fact, on Meta Platforms’ Q4 2024 earnings call, CFO Susan Li implied extended use of Nvidia’s technology as she laid out how the company plans to improve internal cost efficiencies following DeepSeek’s success:

Another way we’re pursuing efficiencies is by extending the useful lives of our servers and associated networking equipment. Our expectation going forward is that we’ll be able to use both our non-AI and AI servers for a longer period of time before replacing them, which we estimate will be approximately 5.5 years. This will deliver savings in annual CapEx and resulting depreciation expense

This throws cold water onto the bullish narrative for AMD. Instead of tech companies purchasing larger volumes of AMD chips amid expected the growth in inferencing workloads, the bulls may be blind sided by more hyper-scalers keeping their existing computing hardware for longer to lower future CapEx budgets. This alternate response to the DeepSeek hype certainly undermines AMD’s revenue growth prospects, and investors should subsequently temper their expectations for big stock price returns over the coming years.

Additionally, while the bulls have emphasized that DeepSeek built its models using Nvidia’s less-powerful H800s as a reason to be optimistic for AMD, they may be missing the fact that the Chinese start-up also relied heavily on Nvidia’s various software extensions to achieve superior model performance. This includes some creative new techniques.

DeepSeek has achieved a breakthrough by leveraging PTX (Parallel Thread Execution), Nvidia’s intermediate representation (IR), to fine-tune performance beyond CUDA’s default compiler optimizations.

By selectively injecting PTX code into their CUDA workflow, DeepSeek is able to achieve greater control over register allocation, instruction scheduling, and memory management, resulting in higher efficiency for AI workloads.

– David Sehyeon Baek, Founder & CEO of Consulting Firm Pygmalion Global (emphasis as per original source)

Meanwhile, on AMD’s recent earnings call, executives struggled to convince investors that its own software layer, ROCm, which is meant to rival Nvidia’s CUDA, is becoming a competitive force to be reckoned with in the post-DeepSeek world.

Contrarily, with the upcoming Nvidia earnings report and conference call later this month, you can bet your bottom dollar that the executives will repeatedly highlight how the chip giant’s entire technology stack was leveraged by DeepSeek to make such giant leaps in AI model advancements. Nvidia executives will be fired up to convince investors that demand for its technology will remain strong in the post- DeepSeek era. This is likely to cause AMD’s stock price to fall further as the market reassess the company’s positioning in the AI revolution.

Furthermore, as if Nvidia’s tight grip over the AI chips market was not enough, AMD stock also faces pressure from the growing trend of hyper-scalers prioritizing custom silicon.

The market had been pricing in the fact that two major CSPs, Amazon’s AWS and Google Cloud, are already fairly self-reliant given that they have been developing their own chips for many years now. So AMD analysts were not expecting high sales to these hyper-scalers anyway.

However, over the past several earnings calls, AMD’s executives had been proclaiming the fact that Meta Platforms is deploying Instinct processors for several types of workloads. This is despite the social media giant also developing its own custom silicon for a few years now.

But amid the emergence of DeepSeek, Meta is doubling down on its in-house chips, particularly the “Meta Training and Inference Accelerator (MTIA),” to appease shareholders that are demanding greater cost efficiencies.

...we’re pursuing cost efficiencies by deploying our custom MTIA silicon in areas where we can achieve a lower cost of compute by optimizing the chip to our unique workloads. In 2024 we started deploying MTIA to our ranking and recommendation inference workloads for ads and organic content. We expect to further ramp adoption of MTIA for these use cases throughout 2025 before extending our custom silicon efforts to training workloads for ranking and recommendations next year.

– Meta CFO Susan Li, Q4 2024 earnings call.

This further dampens the bull case for AMD, as hyper-scalers’ CapEx dollars increasingly flow towards Broadcom (AVGO) and Marvell Technologies (MRVL) amid the custom silicon wave.

It’s becoming increasingly evident that investors should not rely on the hyper-scalers (e.g., Amazon’s AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft & OpenAI, etc.) to drive demand for AMD’s GPUs. Although, with the majority of enterprise workloads still on-premises (instead of cloud platforms), it is this segment where AMD could have an opportunity to gain market share.

In 2024, the CEOs of the major cloud providers frequently cited the fact that 85-90% of workloads remain on-premises. Only time will tell how many of these enterprises will opt to migrate to the cloud amid the AI wave, and how many decide to stay on-prem.

But the truth is, this is a shrinking market segment as more and more enterprises are likely to migrate to the cloud over time. Furthermore, on the recent earnings call, AMD executives did not mention any signs of growing demand for its GPUs from on-prem costumers last quarter, and the lack of concrete AI revenue guidance did not help either.

Bullish Factors

While AMD’s GPU business is struggling to impress investors, the company’s server CPU business has been supportive to data center growth, across both cloud and on-prem customers.

Public cloud demand was also very strong with a number of EPYC instances increasing 27% in 2024 to more than 1,000. AWS, Alibaba, Google, Microsoft and Tencent launched more than 100 AMD general purpose in AI instances in the fourth quarter alone.

We also built significant momentum with Forbes 2000 Global Businesses using EPYC in the cloud, as enterprise customers activated more than double the number of EPYC cloud instances from the prior quarter. This capped-off a strong year of growth as enterprise consumption of EPYC in the cloud nearly tripled from 2023.

Turning to enterprise on-prem adoption. EPYC CPU sales grew by a strong double-digit percentage year-over-year, as sales grew increased, and we closed high-volume deployments with Akamai, Hitachi, LG, ServiceNow, Verizon, Visa and others.

- CEO Lisa Su, AMD Q4 2024 Earnings Call.

Growth in CPU sales revenue may be unflattering to investors, given that the secular growth narrative centers around GPUs as AI continues to evolve going forward. Nonetheless, establishing a solid CPU installed base could prove valuable as AMD strives to translate it into a GPU installed base over time, particularly on the on-premises side.

Although one could argue that this isn’t a strong enough growth narrative, particularly amid competition from the likes of Groq and SambaNova Systems, which also offer alternative GPUs over Nvidia’s chips.

It’s fair to say that AMD does not offer a compelling bullish narrative on the data center side.

Although looking beyond data centers, one key reason investors are opting to hold AMD despite the woes around ‘server GPUs’ is due to the potential for an AI growth narrative in its ‘Client’ segment. More specifically, the bulls believe that AMD could be a big beneficiary once the ‘AI PC wave’ ensues. We covered AMD’s strong positioning to capitalize on this opportunity in a previous article.

While there are little signs of consumers rushing to buy a new PC for advanced AI capabilities yet, AMD continues to deliver market share gains in the overall PC market:

We gained client revenue share for the fourth straight quarter, driven by significantly higher demand for both Ryzen desktop and mobile processors. We had record desktop channel sell-out in the fourth quarter in multiple regions, as Ryzen dominated the best-selling CPU list at many retailers globally. Exceeding 70% share at Amazon, Newegg, MindFactory and numerous others over the holiday period.

Looking into 2025, we are planning for the PC TAM to grow by a mid-single-digit percentage year-on-year. Based on the breadth of our leadership client CPU portfolio and strong design win momentum, we believe we can grow client segment revenue well ahead of the market.

– CEO Lisa Su, AMD Q4 2024 Earnings Call.

Competition from Intel Corporation (INTC) and Qualcomm (QCOM) should certainly not be underestimated, but the opportunity around AI PCs is one of the bright spots for AMD shareholders.

The Investment Case For AMD

AMD’s annual revenue grew by a meager 14% in 2024, while net income increased by 26%, outpacing the top-line growth rate thanks to the company’s commendable efforts to improve operating leverage.

Nonetheless, what investors really want to see is a flourishing AI business that drives top-line sales growth, subsequently trickling down to EPS growth for shareholders.

While AMD’s EPS growth projection is the highest among its chief semiconductor rivals in the GPU space, it isn’t much higher than Nvidia’s expected EPS growth rate, despite AMD growing off a relatively smaller earnings base. Moreover, Nvidia’s EPS growth is mostly being driven by GPU sales growth, which is what investors want to see from AMD.

Now in terms of valuation, while the Forward PE multiple is the most commonly cited metric, a better measure of value would be the Forward Price-Earnings-Growth (Forward PEG) ratio, which adjusts the Forward P/E multiple by the anticipated EPS growth rate.

A ratio of 1x tends to imply that a stock is trading around its fair value. So the bulls have a valid reason to pound the table for AMD given that is trading at a jaw-dropping 0.55x.

Although this ultra-cheap valuation could also be reflective of the fact that the market believes AMD’s EPS growth projections may still be too high. In that case, the stock may not be as cheap as the current multiple may be suggesting, given the risk of estimates being further lowered.

In fact, amid intensifying competition from Nvidia and Broadcom, and rising competition from start-ups like Groq and SambaNova Systems in the AI chips space, it is undermining AMD’s revenue prospects and pricing power. This is pressuring the profit margin outlook as well. Citi even downgraded AMD stock recently:

It appears AMD’s Al revenue is flat to down for the first half of 2025 with margin dilution.

…

the analysts said that they were lowering estimates and downgrading the stock as they expect both downside risk to estimates and a lower multiple given slowing Al growth and margin dilution. – Seeking Alpha News.

Indeed, the market may be hesitant to reward AMD with a higher valuation multiple as the bullish narrative around its AI growth story is falling flat.

Furthermore, while the growing prominence of DeepSeek’s open-source models has been putting pressure on semiconductor stocks, it has induced a more positive outlook for software stocks, which are expected to be the biggest beneficiaries of the declining cost of computing.

There has also been increased interest in software-enabler stocks like Nebius Group (NBIS), as well as the highly anticipated IPO of CoreWeave this year.

So this is the question you must ask yourselves as a growth investor:

Do I really want to park my hard-earned capital in AMD stock given the mounting hurdles to its revenue growth potential, while the AI revolution shifts towards the software segment?

From my perspective, the answer should be no. Instead, I would advise keeping that cash on hand to invest in new, publicly traded software companies.

While the cheap AMD valuation might induce investors to expect big returns, there are plenty of impediments to sales growth that the company will have to overcome which could undermine stock price performance, as discussed throughout the article.

AMD needs a solid AI growth story to see its stock price reverse and deliver attractive returns to shareholders. Until it has one, the stock remains a “hold.”

data.title: Wal-Mart De Mexico S.A.B. de C.V. ADR 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation

data.title: Janus Henderson Mortgage-Backed Securities ETF Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

The U.S. MBS market posted a negative quarterly gain, with the Bloomberg US MBS Index falling 3.16%. The U.S. economy remained resilient, as third quarter 2024 gross domestic product (GDP) growth came in at 2.8% and the unemployment rate held steady around 4.1%. While Treasury markets sold off, risk markets reacted favorably to the decisive Republican Party victory in the November election, due to expectations for expansionary fiscal policies and deregulation. However, after starting the rate-cutting cycle with a 50- basis-point (bps) cut in September, the Federal Reserve (Fed) took a decidedly more hawkish tone at its December meeting, with the revised Fed dot plot implying just 50 bps of rate cuts in 2025. The yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) ended the quarter at 4.57% relative to 3.78% at the end of September. Spreads on agency MBS ended virtually unchanged at 43 bps. The rate on a 30-year conventional mortgage ended higher at 7.28%.

Portfolio review

MBS returns were adversely affected by rising Treasury rates following the November election. Rates markets backed up to account for a potentially slower pace of rate cuts amid the incoming administration’s pro-growth policies.

The portfolio’s longer duration position detracted as rates rose, but we remain positioned for the likely re-steepening of the yield curve.

Security selection within agency mortgage-backed securities contributed, as did an allocation to non-agency mortgages. We increased our allocation to agency floating rate securities to around 10% of the portfolio as we saw the opportunity to capture a high level of coupon income with much lower prepayment risk.

While we believe the MBS asset class is very attractively priced relative to other fixed income sectors, there is significant dispersion among various pools of MBS. Some pools exhibit high prepayment risk, such as recently issued to-be-announced (TBA) mortgages with coupons in the 6%-8% range. In contrast to these TBA securities, we continue to focus on borrower stories within specified mortgage pools, as we seek to acquire mortgages offering attractive yields without elevated levels of prepayment risk.

Manager outlook

As we enter 2025, we believe investors should seek to take advantage of the positive drivers impacting MBS markets: the Fed having initiated its rate-cutting cycle, the likely normalization of rate-spread correlations due to lower inflation, and the defensive characteristics of MBS.

Now that the Fed has begun its rate-cutting cycle and rates markets have priced in a slower pace of cuts, we believe investors in MBS may lean into the yields offered and be less concerned with fighting rising rates. Even if inflation proves to be stickier due to the incoming administration’s policies, we think much of this risk is already priced in.

Real-time data indicates that inflation has continued to moderate toward the central bank’s target of 2%, and we believe that a resurgence of inflation above 3% is unlikely. Rates and spreads have historically been negatively correlated when inflation is below 3% – resulting in reduced portfolio volatility, as any material widening in spreads may be somewhat counterbalanced by falling rates.

The market is currently pricing in a terminal rate around 4%, which is notably higher than the Fed’s estimate of 3.1%. We expect the actual terminal rate to settle at a level lower than 4%, but we also expect the Fed’s estimate to move higher.

Historically, MBS has acted as a ballast during bouts of market volatility, making it a suitable defensive diversifier in multi-asset portfolios, in our view. And just as MBS were more adversely affected by rising rates and high interestrate volatility in the preceding few years, we anticipate that this trend reversing may provide an additional tailwind for returns going forward.

Portfolio management

John Kerschner, CFANick Childs, CFA

Ordinary brokerage commissions apply and will reduce returns.

Returns quoted are past performance and do not guarantee future results; current performance may be lower or higher. Investment returns and principal value will vary; there may be a gain or loss when shares are sold. For the most recent month-end performance call 800.668.0434 or visit Products - US Advisor.

FOR MORE INFORMATION PLEASE VISIT JANUSHENDERSON.COM

Definitions

Basis point (BP) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

Credit quality ratings are measured on a scale that generally ranges from AAA (highest) to D (lowest).

Credit spread is the difference in yield between securities with similar maturity but different credit quality. Widening spreads generally indicate deteriorating creditworthiness of corporate borrowers, and narrowing indicate improving.

Duration measures a bond price’s sensitivity to changes in interest rates. The longer a bond’s duration, the higher its sensitivity to changes in interest rates and vice versa. A yield curve plots the yields (interest rate) of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Typically bonds with longer maturities have higher yields.

10-Year Treasury Yield is the interest rate on U.S. Treasury bonds that will mature 10 years from the date of purchase.

Quantitative Easing (QE) is a government monetary policy occasionally used to increase the money supply by buying government securities or other securities from the market. Monetary tightening refers to central bank activity aimed at curbing inflation and slowing down growth in the economy by raising interest rates and reducing the supply of money.

Please consider the charges, risks, expenses and investment objectives carefully before investing. For a prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus containing this and other information, please call Janus Henderson at 800.668.0434 or download the file from Products - US Advisor. Read it carefully before you invest or send money.

Returns include reinvestment of dividends and capital gains.

OBJECTIVE: Janus Henderson Mortgage-Backed Securities ETF (NYSEARCA:JMBS) seeks a high level of total return consisting of income and capital appreciation.

The opinions are as of 12/31/24, are subject to change and may not reflect the views of others in the organization. Janus Henderson may have a business relationship with certain entities discussed. The comments should not be construed as a recommendation of individual holdings or market sectors, but as an illustration of broader themes.

Holdings are subject to change without notice.

Performance attribution is a method used to analyze and quantify the performance of an investment portfolio relative to its benchmark and does not represent actual performance. Attribution is calculated using gross returns before the deduction of advisory fees and considers differences in weighting for the portfolio and the index. This analysis aims to break down the portfolio's performance into different components to understand what factors contributed to relative return, among other performance characteristics. The analysis can provide insights into which decisions positively or negatively impacted the portfolio's performance and serves as the basis for this discussion.

There is no assurance the stated objective(s) will be met.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal and fluctuation of value.

The Fund will typically enter into“to be announced” or “TBA” commitments when purchasing MBS, which allows the Fund to agree to pay for certain yet-to-be issued securities at a future date and which may have a leveraging effect on the Fund.

The Fund may enter into reverse repurchase agreement transactions and use the cash made available from these transactions to make additional investments in mortgage-related instruments or other fixed-income securities.

Fixed income securities are subject to interest rate, inflation, credit and default risk. The bond market is volatile. As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. The return of principal is not guaranteed, and prices may decline if an issuer fails to make timely payments or its credit strength weakens.

Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) may be more sensitive to interest rate changes. They are subject to extension risk, where borrowers extend the duration of their mortgages as interest rates rise, and prepayment risk, where borrowers pay off their mortgages earlier as interest rates fall. These risks may reduce returns.

Derivatives can be more volatile and sensitive to economic or market changes than other investments, which could result in losses exceeding the original investment and magnified by leverage.

Concentrated investments in a single sector, industry or region will be more susceptible to factors affecting that group and may be more volatile than less concentrated investments or the market as a whole.

Increased portfolio turnover may result in higher expenses and potentially higher net taxable gains or losses.

Actively managed portfolios may fail to produce the intended results. No investment strategy can ensure a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Bloomberg U.S. Mortgage Backed Securities (MBS) Index measures the performance of U.S. fixed-rate agency mortgage backed pass-through securities. Index performance does not reflect the expenses of managing a portfolio as an index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

Janus Henderson Investors US LLC is the investment adviser and ALPS Distributors, Inc. is the distributor. ALPS is not affiliated with Janus Henderson or any of its subsidiaries. Janus Henderson is a trademark of Janus Henderson Group plc or one of its subsidiaries. © Janus Henderson Group plc.
